seo_title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection…
meta_description: Researchers challenge the text-as-prototype paradigm and present an online pseudo-supervised framework to mitigate the modality gap in post-hoc OOD detection.
body_html:
The modality gap in post-hoc out-of-distribution detection with pre-trained vision-language models refers to the misalignment between textual and visual prototypes. This gap cannot be eliminated by prompt engineering alone, according to recent research.

Challenges of Text-as-Prototype Paradigm

Existing methods treat text embeddings of class names as class prototypes, but these prototypes are generally misaligned with the optimal visual prototypes. The modality gap yields an intrinsic limitation that cannot be overcome by prompt engineering alone. Text-as-prototype paradigm is widely adopted, but its limitations have not been thoroughly explored.

Proposed Online Pseudo-Supervised Framework

Visual Feature Space Soft Predictions from Pre-trained VLMs

Directly learns class prototypes in the visual feature space using unlabeled test-time data streams. Soft predictions from pre-trained VLMs are used to guide the online optimization procedure.

Theoretical Guarantees for Convergence Our method achieves theoretical guarantees for the convergence of the online optimization procedure, ensuring that the modality gap is effectively mitigated.

Empirical Results and State-of-the-Art Performance

Our method achieves a new state of the art across a variety of OOD detection setups.

source.title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection with Pre-trained Vision-Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have challenged the common practice of using text embeddings as class prototypes in out-of-distribution (OOD) detection with pre-trained vision-language models, finding that these textual prototypes are often misaligned with optimal visual prototypes due to an intrinsic modality gap. To address this issue, a new online pseudo-supervised framework is proposed that learns class prototypes directly from unlabeled test-time data streams and soft predictions from the pre-trained model. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance across various OOD detection setups.
